→ on-write for bounded audiences (instant reads), on-read for huge audiences (avoid the write storm), hybrid for celebrities
→ assign per-channel monotonic ids and resume from the client's last-seen id over a short event log
→ the dual write isn't atomic, so a crash drifts the two systems
→ outbox for curated domain events, log-based for wholesale replication
→ retained replayable log (pull, offsets) vs delete-on-consume broker (push, per-message routing)
→ lag is the signal: scale consumers (capped at partition count), speed processing, or add partitions
→ by freshness requirement: hours-fine -> batch (simple, exact, high-throughput); seconds-matter -> stream (low-latency, windowed)
→ window by event-time, fire on watermarks, and set an allowed-lateness policy (update / drop / side-output)
→ choreography = event-driven, decoupled, implicit flow; orchestration = central coordinator, explicit, observable
→ intermediate states are visible: dirty reads of a not-yet-compensated charge, lost updates between concurrent sagas
→ cache-aside by default, write-through where the cache must stay warm
→ invalidate on write, TTL as the backstop
→ soft for recoverable, referenced, auditable data; hard for disposable or erasure
→ every read needs the filter, and unique constraints and foreign keys need rethinking

→ JSON column
→ Real columns
→ one table keyed by entity_type plus entity_id, at the cost of a real FK
→ yes, and it is the right tool when the attribute set is variable
→ semi-synchronous usually: sync to one follower for durability, async to the rest for availability
→ quorums: a write needs W acks, a read queries R, and R + W > N forces overlap so the read sees the latest write
→ pin the user's reads to the primary (or a caught-up replica) for a short window after they write
→ strong is a premium paid on every operation: coordination latency, reduced availability under partition, a throughput ceiling
→ from the access patterns: even distribution plus the common queries on a single shard
→ consistent hashing or fixed logical partitions, then dual-write, backfill, verify, cut over
→ it remaps ~(N-1)/N of keys on any node change; consistent hashing moves only ~K/N
→ no -- it evens where keys live, not access frequency; a hot key still lands on one node
→ write-heavy/ingest -> LSM (sequential writes + compaction); read-heavy/transactional -> B-tree (in-place, one lookup)
→ bloom filters skip files lacking the key, leveled compaction bounds files-per-read to ~one per level, block cache serves hot data
→ by the question: membership -> Bloom, distinct-count -> HyperLogLog, frequency/top-K -> Count-Min
→ only if the error direction is harmless for the use, and correctness-critical answers stay exact
→ from the dependency's latency distribution -- above p99 with margin
→ yes -- that's a retry storm; cap with backoff, jitter, budgets, and a circuit-breaker
→ exactly-once effect, not delivery: at-least-once + idempotent processing
→ an atomic key claim (unique constraint) lets exactly one execute; the other replays its result
→ an error rate over a window (min volume), counting only timeouts/5xx/connection errors -- not 4xx
→ a fallback: stale cache, default, queued write, or degraded feature -- not just an error
→ preserve-and-carry the source code by default; degrade to generic only as an explicit, logged fallback
→ whether retrying can succeed (transient) and whether the operation is idempotent (safe to retry)
→ an unbounded queue grows until OOM, and latency collapses as the backlog deepens
→ SLO-driven: shed to protect latency (interactive, loss-tolerant), block/spill to protect completeness (must-not-lose)
→ metric for rates/latency and alerts, log for the detail of one case, trace for where the time went
→ nothing, by design -- calls become no-ops and the request is unaffected
→ read the failures before the storage layer, and dead-letter them so the count becomes X of Y
→ the amplifier is disproportionately loud -- error volume far above the legitimate request rate
→ a proportion of good events over valid events, measured near the user, that moves with user happiness
→ multi-window multi-burn-rate: a fast burn pages, a slow burn tickets

→ yes; make the LB layer redundant (active-passive + floating IP, or active-active behind DNS/anycast)
→ the signal that reflects real saturation: queue depth/lag (workers), RPS/concurrency (web), p99 latency
→ scale-up lag (provisioning + cold start) means it's a step behind; you carry headroom and pre-scale known patterns
→ quorum (a minority can't elect) + lease (a lost leader expires) + fencing token (a stale leader is rejected)
→ shard leadership (one leader per partition, like Kafka) and serve reads from replicas
→ not on its own -- a paused holder can act after expiry; you need a fencing token the resource enforces
→ idempotency (safe to repeat), optimistic concurrency (conditional write), or single-writer partitioning (one owner)
→ active-passive for DR (simple, no write conflicts); active-active / read-local-write-global for global latency
→ fence the old primary before promoting, or use a quorum so a partitioned minority can't write
→ by business domain with a naming convention, so logs are browseable and IAM scopeable
→ cap the function's reserved concurrency to what the downstream can take
→ pull, because you cannot hold connections to a huge intermittent fleet
→ aggregate the job table by state, and reconcile what goes silent
→ safe-by-default: prod read-only, environment explicit, EXPLAIN-checked, elevation logged
→ because the paved path is genuinely faster, and adoption is the real success metric
→ flags allow contradictory combinations nothing prevents; one state plus a table makes only valid states representable
→ a compare-and-swap on the current state, so exactly one wins
→ policy is editable data, changeable without a release
→ reject the requester and the first approver at the second step
→ same machinery; a flag is the boolean toggle, config is any tuned value
→ fall back to the local cache and the safe default
→ additive changes with a tolerant reader; version only to break, with a deprecation window
→ an idempotency key plus a dedupe recorded atomically with the effect
→ token bucket for bursts, sliding window for precision
